subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall structure with introduction, body and conclusion
Logical progression through descriptive statistics, correlation and inferential testing
Relevant figures and tables placed near their discussion
weaknesses: Frequent repetition (e.g., âthere are no outliersâ) reduces conciseness
Typographical artefacts and mislabelled captions interrupt flow
Appendices and citations are not consistently organised
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent, well signposted and organised into clear sections, satisfying the band-3 descriptors for Presentation. Logical gaps and persistent repetition, together with organisational slippage into un-labelled appendices, preclude the top band.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (box-plots, scatter plots, tables)
Explanations accompany most calculations and graphs
Key statistical terminology is employed appropriately
weaknesses: Axis labels and units are sometimes missing or cramped
Occasional abrupt jumps in the narrative (e.g., r-values cited without full working)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student achieves âeffective use of multiple representationsâ and generally clear explanations, meriting the upper half of the band-3 descriptor. Typographical clutter and inconsistency in notation prevent a band-4 award, but the work is stronger than a straight band-2, warranting a 3/4.

strengths: Clear personal motivation linked to Kenyan context
Independent design of stratified sampling approach
Narrative consistently refers back to the studentâs experience
weaknesses: Limited originality in choice of statistical methods (textbook procedures)
Creativity and initiative are modest â standard techniques applied
Independent thinking is meaningful but not outstanding
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach, but creativity and initiative remain within conventional boundaries. This performance aligns with a band-2 award.

strengths: Some reflection on how factors beyond correlation influence crime rates
Brief critical comments at each stage (e.g., interpreting correlation strengths)
weaknesses: Reflection is largely descriptive rather than critical in depth
Connections between the mathematics and personal learning are not fully developed
Sample-size and methodological limitations are only hinted at
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although reflection is limited in depth and criticality, there are recurring evaluative comments and real-world links throughout the report. This satisfies the band-2 descriptor for âmeaningful reflectionâ more than the minimal band-1.

strengths: Relevant SL-level mathematics (box-plots, Pearson/Spearman, ÏÂ²) applied effectively
Mathematics is mostly correct with only minor computational or typographical errors
Understanding of methods and interpretation of results is clear
weaknesses: Occasional mis-types (IQR formula, expected frequency formula)
Some tables (Spearman ranks) are incomplete or mis-labelled
Linear-model critique made before diagnostics are computed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student consistently employs course-level mathematics in an effective manner and achieves mostly correct results, meriting the band-4 award. While there are errors, they are largely minor and do not undermine the overall rigour of the mathematical work.

strandSelections.reasoning: Choosing a question motivated by personal experience of crime and constructing the sampling method shows meaningful independent thought.  The decision to split countries into three development strata and to test several correlation measures is directed by the student rather than a template, evidencing significant â though not outstanding â independent thinking.

Explanations of each statistical tool show the student generally understands what the calculations measure and how to interpret signs and magnitudes.  Minor misconceptions (equating r = â0.29 with âweakâ but later calling â0.55 âvery moderateâ) suggest understanding is good in parts but not thorough or rigorous.

Most numerical values (five-number summaries, r, \chi^2) are plausible, but there are visible errors: the IQR formula is mistyped as $Q_3-Q_3$, ranges for HDI and crime classification are identical despite different scales, and some Pearson calculations in the appendix mis-square values.  These indicate âpartialâ rather than âmostlyâ correct mathematics.

Much of the text is relevant, yet there is frequent repetition (e.g., stating âthere are no outliersâ after every plot) and superfluous narrative (long quotations of formulae already well known).  The document runs to 20+ pages for modest mathematics, so concision is only âmostlyâ present.  Irrelevant typographical artefacts (\overline{ }, stray symbols in tables) further reduce efficiency.

Key statistical terms (quartile, IQR, Spearmanâs coefficient, \chi^2) are used and basic notation is correctly given.  Nonetheless, mathematical typography is inconsistent â â$Q_3-Q_3$â, â\overline{2}â, and misuse of overlines and subscripts appear repeatedly â signalling only âsomeâ appropriate use rather than âmostlyâ or âconsistentâ.

Explanations usually accompany calculations and graphs, allowing the reader to follow the argument, but clarity is hampered by typographical clutter and the occasional abrupt jump (e.g., citing an r-value without showing calculator screen or full working).  Hence communication is âmostly clear with some consistencyâ.

Box-plots, Pearson/Spearman coefficients and a chi-squared contingency test are all squarely within the SL AI syllabus and are applied appropriately to the chosen data.  This satisfies ârelevant mathematics at course level usedâ.  The work does not progress to more advanced inference (e.g., regression diagnostics or confidence intervals), so it does not meet the âused effectivelyâ descriptor of band 4.

The student supplies several representations: three comparative box-plot sets, four scatter diagrams with trendlines, contingency tables and working tables for the correlation coefficients.  These are generally appropriate and help convey ideas, fulfilling the descriptor of âeffective useâ; however, axis labelling is sometimes cramped and duplicated so the work is not at the âsophisticatedâ level.

Reflection is confined to brief statements such as âthe value suggests a moderate negative correlationâ and a short evaluation mentioning other factors that influence crime.  This is indicative of âlimited reflectionâ rather than meaningful or critical reflection.

While the student does use two different correlation statistics and a chi-squared test, these are established textbook techniques for this data type and are applied in a straightforward manner.  There is only limited originality or initiative in the mathematical choices, so creativity is present but modest.

The exploration follows a recognisable path â introduction, data collection, descriptive statistics, correlation measures, test of independence and conclusion â so the reader can follow the argument without major difficulty. Despite some typographical noise, each section builds on the previous one and the student regularly signposts intent (e.g., âThe next step is â¦â).  Logical gaps (for example, prematurely asserting that a linear model is âineffectiveâ before computing r, or mixing â33â and â30â countries) prevent the development from being âexcellentâ, but the text is indisputably coherent and clearly developed overall.

Analysis of results rarely goes beyond restating numerical outcomes; for example, the failure to interrogate discrepancies between Pearson and Spearman or to discuss sample-size limitations.  Consequently, only basic critique is present.

The student briefly notes that âother factors contribute to crimeâ, hinting at links between mathematics and real-world understanding, but there is no sustained discussion of how the mathematics deepened personal understanding.  The connection to understanding is therefore limited.

The report is laid out with a clear introduction, body and conclusion; headers separate the descriptive statistics, correlation work and inferential test.  Tables and graphs usually appear close to the discussion that references them.  However, some material drifts into appendices that are not cleanly labelled and a few figures repeat captions (âdeveloped countries box-plotâ) giving minor organisational weaknesses that preclude the top band.

The narrative repeatedly links the exploration to the studentâs Kenyan context and articulates why each mathematical step was selected.  This demonstrates a clear personal approach that drives the exploration, yet the approach is fairly conventional for a correlation IA and therefore not âhighly personalâ.

annotations.comment: The title is clear but could be more specific by indicating the sample size and scope, for example âCorrelation between HDI and Crime Rate Index among 30 randomly sampled countries.â
The sentence ends abruptly with âForâ and lacks completion of the thought. Ensure each paragraph is complete to maintain coherence.
A stray overline symbol appears outside the main text (\overline{2}). Remove this OCR artifact to improve presentation.
The alternative hypothesis could be more informative by specifying the expected direction of association (e.g., a negative correlation).
An explanation of how the random sample generator was configured (seed, method) would strengthen the reliability discussion and reflect on sampling bias.
Include source citations immediately beneath tables rather than only in the narrative to improve organization and avoid forcing readers to search for references.
The table lists HDI and crime values correctly but decimal alignment varies, hindering quick comparison. Align decimals consistently.
Table 2 header contains garbled symbols and misplaced characters. Recreate the header to clearly label columns.
Refer to one example calculation in the body to demonstrate methodology rather than only stating âused my calculator,â enhancing mathematical communication.
Columns in the five-figure summary table should be precisely labeled Q1, median, Q3, etc., for clarity and consistent notation.
The IQR formula is mistyped as \(Q_3 - Q_3\); it should read \(Q_3 - Q_1\). Correct this to avoid confusion.
Each box-and-whisker plot lacks axis labels and units, making interpretation difficult. Add clear labels to each graph.
The statement âThere are no outliersâ is repeated verbatim across sections. Consolidate this comment to a single summary for conciseness.
Graph caption âGraph 3: Box and whisker plot of developed countriesâ is duplicated. Remove the redundant caption to improve organization.
The caption for the second âGraph 4â is repeated identically. Consolidate and renumber graphs sequentially.
The outlier test narrative is copied from HDI section without adjusting to crime-rate context. Tailor commentary to each dataset.
The short calculation â=5.9â lacks context. Label this result clearly as the range for the given dataset.
The definition of Î£ refers to â\overline{i-1}â incorrectly. Clarify the index notation in the summation.
The claim that a linear model âmight be ineffectiveâ is made before residual or goodness-of-fit analysis. Either compute diagnostics or qualify the statement.
The r-values are inconsistently reported (some with 3 s.f., others without). Standardize significant figures throughout.
The narrative about combined samples is verbose and repeats categories. Condense this section to highlight key insights.
Spearmanâs rank correlation is applied but its formula is not shown. Include the formula to demonstrate full understanding of the method.
The Spearman table for developing countries is empty. Ensure all tables are fully populated to support analysis.
Consider cross-checking rank assignments for Kenya and Azerbaijan, as small errors can affect Spearmanâs coefficient significantly.
The expected frequency formula is given as \((TR-TC)/T\) but should be \(E_{ij}=(\text{row total}\times\text{column total})/\text{grand total}\). Correct for accuracy.
Interpreting the chi-square result against the critical value is well done; it demonstrates correct application of statistical inference.
The conclusion restates correlation but omits discussion of sample size limitations. Reflect on how sample choice could influence results.
The evaluation identifies other crime factors but lacks critical reflection on how the chosen mathematics deepened your understanding. Add this connection.
Several references lack access or publication dates. Review IB citation guidelines to ensure consistency and completeness.
